findings.title: Cosine similarity definition missing ‖z‖ in denominator
findings.snippet: DEFINITION 1: The cosine similarity of two nonzero vectors $\boldsymbol{y}$ and $\boldsymbol{z}$ is $\rho(\boldsymbol{y}, \boldsymbol{z})=\frac{\boldsymbol{y} \cdot \boldsymbol{z}}{\|\boldsymbol{y}\|}$.
findings.explanation: The definition gives ρ(y,z)= (y·z)/‖y‖, but the standard cosine similarity—and the formula used in every proof (e.g., line 490: y*·u^ℓ / (‖y*‖ ‖u^ℓ‖), and line 572)—divides by ‖y‖‖z‖. The denominator is missing ‖z‖. Although eigenvectors satisfy ‖u^ℓ‖=1 so the omission is invisible in most applications, the definition as stated is mathematically wrong for general vectors.

findings.title: Self-referential change of variables in Example 2: b_i defined in terms of b_i
findings.snippet: Performing the change of variables $b_{i}=[\tau-b_{i}] / 2$ and $\beta=-\tilde{\beta} / 2$
findings.explanation: Line 128 writes b_i = [τ − b_i]/2, defining b_i in terms of itself. From context and from footnote 15 (line 735), which correctly writes b_i = [τ − \tilde{b}_i]/2, the tilde on b_i on the right-hand side was dropped. This makes the change of variables circular and undefined.

findings.title: Assumption 3 missing squared norm: C < ‖b̂‖ should be C < ‖b̂‖²
findings.explanation: Two lines earlier (line 198), the text states the first-best is achievable when C ≥ ‖b̂‖². Assumption 3 is meant to rule out this case, so the condition should be C < ‖b̂‖². As written, C < ‖b̂‖ is dimensionally inconsistent with the quadratic cost function K = Σ(b_i − b̂_i)², which has units of b² not b. Footnote 34 (line 770) also confirms the correct threshold is Σ b̂_ℓ² (= ‖b̂‖²).

findings.title: Scalar β incorrectly bolded as 𝛃 in diagonal system
findings.explanation: At lines 175 and 436, the scalar parameter β is typeset in bold (\boldsymbol{β}), suggesting it is a vector or matrix. In every other occurrence (equations (2), (3), (4), Theorem 1, all proofs), β is an unbolded scalar. The bold is applied only when β appears between two bold matrices I and Λ, likely a copy-paste artifact.

findings.title: Missing boldface on vectors in equation (2) and line 166
findings.explanation: Equation (2) writes I, a*, and b without boldface, but equation (3) immediately after writes [𝐈−β𝐆]⁻¹𝐛 with full boldface. Similarly, line 166 mixes bolded U, Λ with unbolded I, a*, b. The paper's convention (established in §2) is that matrices and vectors are bold.
findings.fix: Write $[\boldsymbol{I}-\beta \boldsymbol{G}] \boldsymbol{a}^{*}=\boldsymbol{b}$ in equation (2), and similarly in line 166.

findings.title: Missing boldface on y* in Theorem 1 and similarity ratio definition
findings.explanation: The vector y* is defined with bold on line 203 as 𝐲* = 𝐛* − 𝐛̂ and appears bolded in all other places (lines 239, 295, 504). However, in the statement of Theorem 1 (line 214) and the definition of the similarity ratio r*_ℓ (line 249), y* appears without bold, breaking the convention for vectors.

findings.title: Underline notation inconsistently dropped on b̂_ℓ in principal-component basis
findings.explanation: The paper defines z̲ = U⊤z (line 163) to denote projections in the principal-component basis. In the reformulated problem (line 227), b̲̂_ℓ is correctly underlined. But in Theorem 1's own equation (6) (line 220), and throughout the proof's Lagrangian (line 465), FOC (line 471), budget condition (line 483), and lines 491, 532–557, the underline is dropped and the symbol is written as b̂_ℓ. Since b̂_i (subscript i) denotes the i-th individual's status quo return in the original basis, the missing underline creates genuine ambiguity about which basis is intended.
findings.fix: Consistently write $\underline{\hat{b}}_{\ell}$ (with underline) whenever the subscript is ℓ and the quantity is a principal-component projection.

findings.title: Lagrangian missing square on b̲̂_ℓ in objective term
findings.explanation: In the Lagrangian (line 465), the first sum has \underline{\hat{b}}_{ℓ} without a square, while the objective being optimized (lines 451–452) has \underline{\hat{b}}_{ℓ}². The square is required; without it the Lagrangian does not match the objective and the FOC derivation does not follow.
findings.fix: Change $\underline{\hat{b}}_{\ell}$ to $\underline{\hat{b}}_{\ell}^{2}$ in the first sum of the Lagrangian.

findings.title: Denominator ordering reversed: α_{n-1}/(α_{n-1}−α_n) vs. Proposition 2's α_{n-1}/(α_n−α_{n-1})
findings.snippet: the term $\alpha_{n-1} /(\alpha_{n-1}-\alpha_{n})$ of the inequality is large when $\lambda_{n-1}-\lambda_{n}$...is small.
findings.explanation: Line 301 discusses the bound from Proposition 2 part 2 (line 296), which contains (α_{n-1}/(α_n − α_{n-1}))². For β<0, α_n > α_{n-1}, so α_n − α_{n-1} > 0 and the Proposition's expression is positive. However, the text writes α_{n-1}/(α_{n-1} − α_n), which is negative. Although the bound squares the term (hiding the sign), the text discusses it unsquared as being 'large', which is incoherent for a negative quantity.

findings.title: Eigenvectors u^n, u^{n-1} called 'maximizers' of minimization problems
findings.snippet: Moreover, the eigenvector $\boldsymbol{u}^{n}$ is a maximizer of the first problem, while $\boldsymbol{u}^{n-1}$ is a maximizer of the second
findings.explanation: Line 324 refers to the problems on line 321–322, which define λ_n and λ_{n-1} via min, not max. The eigenvectors u^n and u^{n-1} are therefore minimizers of the corresponding Rayleigh quotient problems. Compare with line 316, which correctly says u^1 is a 'maximizer' of the λ_1 = max problem.
findings.fix: Replace 'maximizer' with 'minimizer' in both instances on line 324.

findings.title: Eigenvector entry subscript/superscript reversed: u^i_ℓ vs. u^ℓ_i
findings.explanation: The paper's convention (established at line 186: a*_i = Σ_ℓ (1/(1−βλ_ℓ)) u^ℓ_i b̲_ℓ) is that u^ℓ_i means the i-th entry of the ℓ-th eigenvector (superscript = eigenvector index, subscript = node index). Line 584 writes u^i_ℓ, reversing the indices. While U is orthogonal so numerically U_{iℓ}=U_{ℓi} is false in general, this notation swap is confusing.

findings.title: Spurious asterisk on α₂ in proof of Proposition 2
findings.explanation: Line 543 writes α₂* (with asterisk), but α*_ℓ is never defined; the asterisk likely crept in from the adjacent x*_ℓ terms. The derivation (line 557) yields the factor (2α₁ − α₂), with no asterisk on α₂. The next line (544) correctly bounds this by 2, confirming the intended quantity is just α₂.

findings.title: Double-hat \hat{\hat{b}}_ℓ² in definition of D (proof of Prop 2)
findings.explanation: Line 535 has \hat{\hat{b}}_ℓ² (double hat). No symbol with two hats is defined anywhere. From context (matching W^s on line 526), this should be \underline{\hat{b}}_ℓ² (underline + single hat).

findings.title: Transpose/asterisk misplaced in expected welfare formula
findings.explanation: Line 353 writes (a̲⊤)* instead of (a̲*)⊤. The asterisk denotes the equilibrium and should be on a̲ before the transpose is applied, i.e. (a̲*)⊤(a̲*). As written, the asterisk appears to be applied to the already-transposed vector, which is notationally anomalous.

findings.title: Prime on α rendered as superscript rather than subscript: α'_ℓ vs α_{ℓ'}
findings.explanation: Line 498 writes α'_ℓ (prime as superscript on α with subscript ℓ) in the numerator of the second fraction, but the intended quantity is α_{ℓ'} (subscript ℓ'). The left-hand side correctly uses ℓ' as a subscript on r*, and α_{ℓ'} appears correctly in the first fraction's denominator. The misplaced prime on α could be read as a derivative.

findings.title: Missing summation index i in cost function (10)
findings.explanation: Line 384 writes Σ_{∈𝒩} with no index variable before the ∈ symbol. The surrounding text discusses 'the sum of the variances of the b_i', confirming the missing index is i.

findings.title: New symbol Δb* introduced without definition in proof of Proposition 2
findings.explanation: Lines 572 and 596 use Δb* to denote b* − b̂, but this symbol is never formally defined. The rest of the paper (and the definition on line 203) uses y* = b* − b̂ for this quantity. The proof itself expands Δb* as (b* − b̂), confirming it is just y*, making the new notation unnecessary and potentially confusing.
findings.fix: Replace $\Delta \boldsymbol{b}^{*}$ with $\boldsymbol{y}^{*}$ to match the notation established in Section 4.
